retrieved_docs.title: Leiomyosarcoma -- Medical Oncology -- Pazopanib
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple anti-angiogenic agents, such as bevacizumab, sorafenib, sunitinib, pazopanib, vandetanib, DC-101, and TNP-470, have shown anti-sarcoma activity in mouse models. Few of these drugs showed clinical activity in phase 1 trials; however, the success of most of these drugs could not be replicated in phase 2 trials. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Trials -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Phase 0 trials are optional, exploratory, first-in-human trials that are designed to determine whether the drug affects human subjects as expected from earlier pre-clinical investigations. These trials involve the administration of low, nontherapeutic doses of the study drug to a small number of participants (typically 10 to 15) and help gather preliminary data on the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of the drug in human subjects. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Plasmablastic Lymphoma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Several ongoing trials are pertinent to PBL. These are listed below, along with the phase and current status. Belantamab mafodotin in plasmablastic lymphoma and ALK+ large B-cell lymphoma (NCT04676360, phase 2, recruiting) Pomalidomide and dose-adjusted EPOCH with and without rituximab for HIV-associated lymphomas (NCT05389423, phase 1, recruiting) A study of daratumumab and dose-adjusted EPOCH in plasmablastic lymphoma (NCT04139304, phase 1, recruiting)

retrieved_docs.title: Leiomyosarcoma -- Medical Oncology -- Eribulin (E7389)
retrieved_docs.content: Eribulin mesylate (E7389) is an analog of halichondrin B approved for metastatic breast cancer. Eribulin mesylate blocks the G2 phase and the M phase of the cell cycle through a tubulin-based antimitotic mechanism. This blockage inhibits the spindle formation in the cell cycle, leading to cancer cell apoptosis. [62] The FDA has approved eribulin for patients with unresectable or metastatic liposarcoma whose tumor progressed after anthracycline chemotherapy, based on the results of the phase 3 trial (E7389-G000-309). [63] In a pre-planned subset analysis of the phase 3 trial results, the patients diagnosed with leiomyosarcoma showed comparable efficacy to single-agent dacarbazine. The median overall survival for eribulin versus dacarbazine (12.7 months versus 13.0 months, respectively (HR, 0.93 [95% CI, 0.71 to 1.20]; P = .57) and the median progression-free survival (2.2 months versus 2.6 months, respectively, (HR, 1.07 [95% CI, 0.84 to 1.38]; P = .58) and objective response rate (5% versus 7%) were not significantly different. [64] The FDA has not yet approved the drug for leiomyosarcoma. However, it provides a reasonable option for patients with leiomyosarcoma. Eribulin is administered intravenously at a dosage of 1.4 mg/m² over 2 to 5 minutes on Days 1 and 8 of a 21-day cycle. Close monitoring of liver and renal function is necessary with appropriate dose adjustments in patients with impairment of either of the two. Adverse effects of eribulin include neutropenia, peripheral nerve neuropathy, and QTc prolongation.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Trials -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: In phase II clinical trials, the experimental drug testing takes place within a larger group of participants (typically 100 to 300) to determine its efficacy and to further evaluate its safety. Phase II trials usually last for months and involve participants with the target disease or condition. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Stargardt Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: The investigation of several drugs that can reduce lipofuscin accumulation is currently underway. Reducing lipofuscin can be achieved by either inhibiting the retinoid cycle by inhibiting the key enzymes (emixustat and isotretinoin) or disrupting the transporters like retinol-binding protein 4 (RBP4) to decrease the toxic bisretinoid formation (fenretinide, A1120, BPN-14136, STG-001, LBS-008 or tinlarebant). [61] These drugs include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Trials -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Phase I drug trials screen the investigational drug for safety. These trials help determine a safe dose range and identify any side effects associated with the new drug. Drug testing occurs over a few weeks or a month in a small group of healthy volunteers (usually 20 to 80). [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Photodynamic Therapy for Dermatologic Conditions -- Indications -- Nonmelanoma Skin Cancers
retrieved_docs.content: There have been multiple clinical trials that focus on the efficacy of PDT in the treatment of basal cell carcinoma. In a phase III clinical trial comparing the safety and efficacy of BF-200 ALA gel with BF-200 MAL cream in the treatment of basal cell carcinoma, 93.4% of the patients in the BF-200 ALA group achieved clearance after 12 weeks compared to  91.8% of the patients in the BF-200 MAL group. [3] This study was pivotal in demonstrating the similar efficacies of ALA and MAL when used in conjunction with PDT in the treatment of basal cell carcinoma. Additionally, the frequency and severity of treatment-emergent adverse events were comparable between the ALA and MAL groups, with patients reporting mild-to-moderate intensity adverse events. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Trials -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Investigator-initiated or academic clinical trials are clinical studies initiated and conducted by a non-pharmaceutical company researcher, who could be an individual investigator, a collaborative study group, or an institution. The key premise in this type of clinical trial is that the responsibility of being the sponsor as well as the investigator may lie with the person who designs and conducts the study, though sponsors sometimes fund these trials, especially if they think it may lend support for a new indication. Investigator-initiated trials help generate data on the safety and effectiveness of a drug in the real-world setting. They are largely driven by questions that remain unanswered from phases I to III of drug trials. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Other pertinent studies include trials that supported the first-line treatment with imatinib and dosing including the CML Study IV, [13] the Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Optimization and Selectivity (TOPS) study, [14] and the International Randomized Study of Interferon and STI571 trial (IRIS). [22] Important trials concerning the efficacy and dosing of the second generation FDA-approved tyrosine kinase inhibitors include Bosutinib Efficacy and Safety in Newly Diagnosed CML trial (BELA), [15] Bosutinib Versus Imatinib in Adult Patients With Newly Diagnosed Chronic Phase Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia trial (BFORE), [23] Dasatinib Versus Imatinib Study in Treatment-Naive Chronic Myeloid Leukemia Patients trial (DASISION), [16] and the ENESTnd study. [17] Pertinent studies and ongoing trials assessing tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy discontinuation efficacy and candidate recommendations include the imatinib discontinuation study (STIM1), [24] Evaluating Nilotinib Efficacy and Safety in Clinical Trials: Treatment-Free Remission study (ENESTfreedom), [25] Imatinib Suspension and Validation study (ISAV), [26] and Discontinuation of Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Therapy in Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia trial (EURO-SKI). [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Trials -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Drug trials in human subjects are generally subdivided into five phases, with each phase comprised of at least one distinct clinical trial. The drug is usually approved for use in the general population by the national regulatory authority if it successfully passes through drug trial phases I, II, and III. The entire drug development process usually takes several years to proceed through all the phases.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: A search of ClinicalTrials.gov shows there are 221 active, interventional CML trials with 125 recruiting. These trials are widely variable, with study topics covering novel drugs, treatment optimization of current treatment modalities, potential drug treatment combination regimens, the efficacy of treatment discontinuation, and immunotherapy. Ongoing trials of note include the randomized international phase 3 study Evaluating Nilotinib Efficacy and Safety in Clinical Trials-Newly Diagnosed Patients (ENESTnd) [17] , and the phase 3 study Results of Efficacy and Safety of Radotinib Compared with Imatinib in Newly Diagnosed Chronic Phase Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (RERISE). [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Brodalumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to plaque psoriasis, researchers have explored brodalumab as a potential treatment for other auto-inflammatory diseases. A phase 2 randomized control trial examining the efficacy of brodalumab for the treatment of psoriatic arthritis (PsA) showed that by week 12, almost 40% of patients treated with brodalumab (at either 140 mg or 280 mg doses) were able to achieve 20% improvement in the American College of Rheumatology response criteria (ACR 20) compared to 18% in the placebo group. By 24 weeks, 51% of patients in the 140 mg brodalumab group and 64% of those in the 280 mg group achieved improved ACR 20 response. The research revealed no statistical difference in response among patients who had received prior treatment with a biologic medication.

retrieved_docs.title: Ixazomib -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Ixazomib is also currently being studied in a phase II clinical trial involving patients with Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia. [5] Many studies are underway with ixazomib for use in bladder cancer, T-cell lymphomas, and neuroblastomas; none have been approved yet. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Regulation of Medication Production -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: An entrant must produce evidence (under 505(d)) that the medicine is safe and efficacious when requesting clearance for an NDA. Animal and clinical (human) investigations are required as evidence. Section 505(a) prohibits the distribution of any novel medicine unless it has been FDA approved. 505(i), which permits the FDA to exempt a medication from the NDA criteria for clinical research, avoids this seemingly conflicting position. The maker must file a "Notice of Claimed Investigational Exemption for a New Drug," also known as an Investigational New Drug (IND) Application, to get this exclusion. The producer could then perform clinical tests of its IND if it is authorized. The filing of an IND follows the applicant's lengthy preclinical inquiry. The petitioner has decided that the drug has prospective worth and would be reasonably safe to try in people through laboratory experiments and animal testing. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Keratoconjunctivitis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Key Pertinent Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Lifitegrast Clinical Trials (OPUS-1, OPUS-2, OPUS-3): These clinical trials investigated the efficacy and safety of lifitegrast, an antagonist of lymphocyte function-associated antigen 1 (LFA-1), in reducing DED signs and symptoms. Significant improvements in ocular surface staining and patient-reported symptoms were observed, supporting its approval for the treatment of moderate-to-severe dry eye. [96]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1522
retrieved_docs.content: Betibeglogene autotemcel is classified as a biologické přípravky, meaning it is a biological product (biologic). This categorization places betibeglogene autotemcel among therapeutics derived from biological sources, which influences its development, regulatory considerations, and clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310
retrieved_docs.content: Lixisenatide is classified as a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue, belonging to the therapeutic class of GLP‑1 analogues that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1 to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, and slow gastric emptying. As a member of this drug class, lixisenatide is employed in the management of type 2 diabetes mellitus, often in combination with other antidiabetic agents, and its pharmacologic actions are consistent with those of other GLP‑1 analogues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 749
retrieved_docs.content: Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders [TC] is the therapeutic class that includes both tromethamin and betibeglogene autotemcel. Betibeglogene autotemcel is further identified as a biologic preparation (biologické přípravky) in its source mapping. By grouping tromethamin and betibeglogene autotemcel under the Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders therapeutic class, clinicians recognize that both agents serve as volume‑expanding modifiers of blood products, although they differ in composition, with tromethamin being a small‑molecule drug and betibeglogene autotemcel being a biologically derived therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2560
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists are classified within the broader category of hormones and related agents, reflecting their role in modulating the endocrine system. Within this class, triptorelin represents a specific member, exemplifying the pharmacologic application of LHRH agonists in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 772
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues are a class of incretin‑based medications that include both exenatid and lixisenatide. Exenatid, as a member of the GLP‑1 analogue family, acts on the GLP‑1 receptor to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, and slow gastric emptying. Lixisenatide similarly belongs to the GLP‑1 analogue class and shares these pharmacologic effects. In addition to being an individual GLP‑1 analogue, lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin glargine/lixisenatide, which pairs basal insulin glargine with the GLP‑1 receptor agonist to provide combined basal insulin and incretin therapy for improved glycemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes. This integration underscores the therapeutic strategy of using GLP‑1 analogues both as standalone agents and as components of combination regimens to optimize blood glucose management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 425
retrieved_docs.content: the experimental compound PD‑134308 functions simultaneously as a hormonal antagonist and an antitumor agent directed against the target isa, exemplifying dual pharmacologic activity. This integrated framework delineates classifications, qualifying relationships, therapeutic scopes and specific contraindications of key antineoplastic and inhibitory drugs, supporting informed oncologic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 218
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class DRUGS USED IN DIABETES is organized into two major subclasses: INSULINS AND ANALOGUES, which are injectable agents that replace or augment endogenous insulin, and BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS, which comprise oral and non‑insulin injectable agents that lower plasma glucose by distinct mechanisms. Within the insulin subclass, long‑acting basal formulations such as INSULIN GLARGINE, INSULIN DEGLUDEC/LIRAGLUTIDE (combination of insulin degludec with the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutide), and INSULIN GLARGINE/LIXISENATIDE (combination of insulin glargine with the GLP‑1 analogue lixisenatide) are classified as INSULINS AND ANALOGUES FOR INJECTION, LONG‑ACTING. Insulins that are intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting agents are also included in this subclass. The non‑insulin glucose‑lowering subclass is further divided by mechanism of action: ALPHA‑GLUCOSIDASE INHIBITORS (e.g., benfluorex) delay carbohydrate absorption; DIPEPTIDYL PEPTIDASE‑4

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1540
retrieved_docs.content: Domperidone, a dopamine‑antagonist medication commonly used for its anti‑emetic and gastrointestinal pro‑kinetic effects, may have its associated adverse effects or reduced efficacy mitigated when a substantial, heavy breakfast (فطار غزوي) is consumed, suggesting that taking the drug with a large morning meal can help prevent potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 112
retrieved_docs.content: organized under “blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins” and include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors (alogliptin, sitagliptin), and GLP‑1 analogues (liraglutide, lixisenatide). A further subclass, biguanides, comprises metformin and fenformin, which share a common chemical scaffold and glucose‑lowering activity. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how glycerol is shared across diverse formulations and how diabetes medications are systematically categorized by mechanism, molecular target, and pharmacologic profile to guide clinical use and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropoietin and glucagon are both classified as members of the broader category “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein,” indicating that each is a peptide‑based biomolecule. As a peptide hormone, glucagon can be managed therapeutically by an approach described as “رد فعل تأقي,” suggesting that this specific type of counter‑reaction or antagonistic intervention may be used in the treatment of conditions involving glucagon.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues are a class of antidiabetic agents that includes both exenatid and liraglutid. In addition to being a member of the GLP‑1 analogue class, liraglutid is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide, which pairs basal insulin degludec with the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutid to provide complementary mechanisms for glucose lowering. This relationship highlights how individual GLP‑1 analogues can serve both as standalone therapies and as components of combination regimens designed to enhance glycemic control in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 114
retrieved_docs.content: with uterine lining tumor (ورم بطانة الرحم), whereas leuprolide may be used to treat the same condition, illustrating opposite therapeutic roles. This interconnected network of drug classes, active ingredients, receptor targets, therapeutic indications, contraindications, and excipients underlies the pharmacologic management of gastrointestinal disorders, hormonal contraception, and certain oncologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2534
retrieved_docs.content: Administration – action encompasses specific therapeutic approaches, including the administration of prophylactic beta blocker and beta‑adrenergic receptor blocking agent therapy, both of which are methods used to deliver beta‑blockade for preventive or treatment purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 267
retrieved_docs.content: insulin degludec/liraglutide contains the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutide, and insulin glargine/lixisenatide contains the GLP‑1 analogue lixisenatide. This integration of an insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue provides both basal insulin coverage and incretin‑mediated glucose control in a single injection. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies how diabetes pharmacotherapy is organized: insulin‑based therapies are distinguished from non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents, and within each group, drugs are further classified by mechanism, duration of action, and formulation, facilitating precise selection and combination of therapies for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1154
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) Agonist is classified as a hormone‑related agent and belongs to the leuprolide family of compounds. Within this family, specific agents such as triptorelin are recognized as members of the LHRH Agonist group. The broader category of L [Preparations] serves as the parent compound from which leuprolide derivatives are derived. Clinically, leuprolide and its related LHRH agonists are employed in the therapeutic management of uterine endometrial tumors (endometrial carcinoma), highlighting their role in endocrine‑driven cancer treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic landscape for diabetes is organized into two principal categories: insulins and analogues, and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Within the insulin subclass, formulation groups are defined. “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting” comprises rapid‑acting analogue insulin aspart (human), which binds the human insulin receptor (INSR) and produces the physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting an anabolic action on adipose tissue. “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting” includes insulin glargine and the fixed‑dose combinations insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide; both combinations integrate a basal insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue to enhance glycaemic control. “Insulin, Beef” is a protein entity classified within the same combined intermediate‑/long‑acting‑with‑fast‑acting group, illustrating the diversity of insulin sources.
